critique.summary: GrapheneOS announced that its operating system will remain usable globally without requiring personal information or accounts, though device availability may be restricted by regional regulations. The OS currently supports Pixel devices and plans to expand support to future Motorola devices through a new partnership, maintaining strict hardware security requirements.
mainPoints: GrapheneOS can be used without personal information, identification, or accounts worldwide.
Device sales may be restricted in some regions due to local regulations.
Currently, GrapheneOS supports Pixel devices, including Pixel 6a until its end-of-life in 2027.
GrapheneOS has a long-term partnership with Motorola to support future devices meeting its security standards.
GrapheneOS does not support devices lacking necessary hardware security features or driver/firmware updates.
Samsung devices are not supported due to locked bootloaders and security restrictions preventing alternative OS installation.
GrapheneOS emphasizes privacy and security, requiring hardware standards similar to or exceeding industry norms.
critique.truthfulness: The claims made in the article align with publicly available information from GrapheneOS's official communications and reputable tech news sources. GrapheneOS is known for its focus on privacy and security, supporting only devices that meet strict hardware and software criteria, primarily Google Pixel phones. The announced partnership with Motorola to support future devices is corroborated by external Motorola news sources. The limitations regarding Samsung devices and the requirement to avoid personal information are consistent with GrapheneOS's documented policies and technical constraints. Therefore, the article's claims are accurate and truthful.
considerations: The article does not discuss the potential impact of regional regulatory changes on user access in detail.
It omits mention of the technical challenges and user expertise required to install and maintain GrapheneOS.
There is no discussion about the trade-offs users face when choosing GrapheneOS over more mainstream operating systems, such as app compatibility limitations.
The article does not address how GrapheneOS compares to other privacy-focused operating systems like LineageOS or CalyxOS.
Potential risks or limitations related to future Motorola device support and timelines are not explored.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers around GrapheneOS's privacy features, legal challenges related to age verification laws, and practical usability concerns such as app compatibility and partnerships. Users share personal experiences, legal observations, and concerns about the impact of legislation on privacy-focused operating systems.

comments.author: diowldxiks
comments.text:
I did the switch to graphene on my pixel 9 pro recently and have 0 regrets. it's just a better OS than the google infected android. Here's what I did:

* Follow instructions to install graphene on their website: https://grapheneos.org/install/

* Set up a private space which will be used for google play services required apps (bank stuff, etc). Install google play and google play services in the private space. Do not install google play services on your main profile. Set the private space to lock after 5 mins of inactivity. Set up google play on a brand new google account. You'll need to provide a phone number during setup. I used my normal phone number, others who are more concerned about deanonymization could use rental phone numbers or other things. Install any apps into the private space.

* Try to install apps on your main profile, ideally open source, privacy respecting stuff. Some recent apps I've found that work great and replace google infested stuff - AntennaPod for podcasts, OrganicMaps for OSM maps, Obsidian for notetaking (google keep), KOReader for ebooks, Molly/Signal for messaging. Vanadium as the default browser works well, except it doesn't have adblock plus for youtube (it does some other ad blocking though and works fine).

Things I still don't have a great solution for:

* Android auto - I don't think it works from a private space due to auto locking. Still figuring this out

* Spotify - since it also needs to run in the background and I haven't found a better music replacement.

Overall graphene has been a far better experience and I like it much more, and feel more in control of my hardware.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a detailed, practical user experience with GrapheneOS, including setup tips and app recommendations, making it a reliable and informative perspective.

comments.author: matheusd
comments.text:
Unfortunately, it doesn't look like this is sufficient.

While I had great success with GrapheneOS in the past, bank apps in Brazil have started blocking it, even when the profile you run it under has Google services installed. So GrapheneOS (again, even with all Google Play Services and all other dependencies installed in a given profile) is still not completely transparent to apps.

This may be a coincidence (as I don't use it every day), but I noticed blocking started just as the recent Felca Law (which introduced mandatory age verification for every software, app and OS in Brazil) came into effect.
comments.analysis: This comment highlights real-world challenges with app compatibility and legal impacts, providing a nuanced view of GrapheneOS's limitations under new regulations.

comments.author: joecool1029
comments.text:
One of the reasons I build my own LineageOS builds is because of terrible one-party consent recording laws (in places like California) there’s no geographic way in Android to check it on a state-by-state way. It just goes off country code and disables it for the US since quite a few states it’s illegal to do. For my state it isn’t illegal so I modified my builds to allow it.

There are other things like this too in Android disabled on per-country. Japan has a camera shutter noise that cannot be disabled but this was a request by their carriers, apparently not a law, big discussion under this review: https://review.lineageos.org/c/LineageOS/android_frameworks_...
comments.analysis: This comment offers knowledgeable insights about regional legal restrictions affecting Android features, adding valuable context to the discussion.

comments.author: Sophira
comments.text:
I have to wonder how this will impact their partnership with Motorola. Presumably, Motorola will have more difficulty if they're found not to be complying with relevant law...

I hope GrapheneOS isn't completely banking on their partnership succeeding. If Motorola devices ever became the only devices that GrapheneOS works on, and it's being done with Motorola's blessing, then it could be more easily legislated out of existence.
comments.analysis: Raises a valid concern about the sustainability of GrapheneOS's partnership with Motorola and potential legal risks, though speculative.

comments.author: bivlked
comments.text: the commitment to not requiring google play services is what makes this different from most privacy ROMs. the real question is whether the app ecosystem holds - banking apps and 2FA are always the pain point that pushes people back to stock android.
comments.analysis: Accurately identifies a key challenge for privacy OSes: app compatibility, especially with banking and 2FA apps, which is a common barrier for adoption.

comments.author: logdahl
comments.text: Of course :^) I'm close to jumping ship to GrapheneOS, but as a Swedish resident I really need our digital id services, digital mailbox, and banking apps. I have seen their page on app support, but I am slightly afraid its not up to date / will break any time. I guess the solution is to use one banking android phone and one GrapheneOS for everyday use.
comments.analysis: Expresses practical concerns about app support and regional service compatibility, reflecting common user considerations.

comments.author: blacksmith_tb
comments.text:
I appreciate the principled stand, but on the other hand the CA law only requires users to self-identify when setting up accounts (and then the OS will expose age to apps), that seems fairly toothless (though wrongheaded) compared to TX and UT wanting to scan photo IDs[1]

1: https://www.tomshardware.com/software/operating-systems/cali...
comments.analysis: Provides a measured legal perspective on California's age verification law, contrasting it with more invasive laws in other states.

comments.author: shevy-java
comments.text: Good. It is time to get rid of those corporate lobbyists that try to sniff for user data and then write up corporate laws. I would not understand in the slightest why my computer should provide any information about myself to the outside world - so why is the law suddenly changed? Who, aside from Meta, is pushing for this? Clearly the "but but but protect the kids!" is the red herring here. The whole law could have been worded differently than it was - that was not "accidental".
comments.analysis: Expresses a strong opinion against corporate influence on privacy laws, though somewhat speculative about motivations.

comments.author: RRRA
comments.text: Canadians not being able to disable Amber alerts sent at presidential level all the time might also be interested to be able to sleep again...
comments.analysis: Brief comment highlighting a specific annoyance related to emergency alerts, relevant but less substantive.

comments.author: calvinmorrison
comments.text: so what is going to happen? Will California issue slave catcher warrants for those who violate laws? will Free Stater sheriffs dispatch citizens on long haul flights to meet their fate in the Golden State?
comments.analysis: This comment uses hyperbole and sarcasm, detracting from constructive discussion and accuracy.
